title: Large Language Model probabilities cannot distinguish between possible and impossible language

en.general:
The study examines whether large language models (LLMs) can tell the difference between possible and impossible language by analyzing their internal probability scores. The researchers tested four models by comparing how they assign probabilities to different types of sentences: grammatical sentences, less common grammatical sentences, ungrammatical sentences, and sentences that are odd in meaning or context.

The key idea was that if LLMs truly understand grammatical limits, their probability scores should show a clear difference when they encounter ungrammatical sentences compared to other types of odd sentences. The researchers expected ungrammatical sentences to stand out with much higher surprisal—a measure of how unexpected a sentence is to the model.

However, the results did not support this prediction. Instead of ungrammatical sentences showing the highest surprisal, sentences that were semantically or pragmatically odd (meaning strange or contextually inappropriate) consistently had higher surprisal rates. This suggests that LLMs do not treat ungrammatical sentences as uniquely problematic compared to other types of linguistic oddities.

The findings imply that the probability scores from LLMs may not reliably reflect their internal understanding of grammatical rules. Therefore, claims about LLMs distinguishing possible from impossible language should be tested using methods other than probability-based evaluations.
en.methodology:
The research design focuses on evaluating whether large language models (LLMs) can distinguish between possible and impossible language by analyzing their internal probability distributions. The study employs a novel benchmark to elicit probabilities from four LLMs, computing minimal-pair surprisal differences across four conditions: lower frequency grammatical sentences, ungrammatical sentences, semantically odd sentences, and pragmatically odd sentences. The core prediction is that if string-probabilities reflect grammatical limits, ungrammatical sentences should exhibit uniquely high surprisal compared to other linguistic violations.

The model/algorithm choices involve selecting four LLMs to generate probability distributions for the benchmark sentences. The evaluation strategy relies on comparing surprisal differences between grammatical and ungrammatical conditions, as well as other types of linguistic oddities. The study assumes that surprisal, derived from model probabilities, can serve as a proxy for syntactic knowledge, though this assumption is explicitly tested.

Baselines include grammatical sentences as a reference point, with comparisons drawn against lower frequency grammatical sentences, ungrammatical sentences, semantically odd sentences, and pragmatically odd sentences. The evaluation measures the extent to which each condition deviates from the grammatical baseline in terms of surprisal.

The results indicate that ungrammatical sentences do not exhibit a unique surprisal signature compared to semantically or pragmatically odd sentences, suggesting that string-probabilities do not reliably capture model-internal representations of syntactic knowledge. This finding challenges prior claims about LLMs' ability to distinguish grammatical from ungrammatical language, advocating for alternative methodological approaches to assess syntactic competence in models.
en.dataset:
Language(s): English

Source: Novel benchmark designed by the authors, using model-internal representations from 4 large language models (specific models not listed in the abstract)

Size: Not explicitly stated in the abstract

Modality: Text (linguistic stimuli)

Labels: Grammatical sentences, lower frequency grammatical sentences, ungrammatical sentences, semantically odd sentences, pragmatically odd sentences

Metadata: Not explicitly detailed in the abstract (assumed to include sentence types, frequency data, and model-specific representations)

Splits: Not explicitly mentioned in the abstract (assumed to include separate conditions for grammatical vs. ungrammatical comparisons)
